coredata.dc:title: Design of Fe3O4@SiO2@mSiO2-organosilane carbon dots nanoparticles: Synthesis and fluorescence red-shift properties with concentration dependence
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Xiaolei
Wang, Wei
Li, Qianli
Lin, Han
Xu, Yue
Zhuang, Lin
coredata.dc:description: Organosilane-functionalized carbon dots (SiCDs) immobilized silica composite nanoparticles (Fe3O4@SiO2@mSiO2-SiCDs) with dually emissive properties have been prepared by a modified sol-gel method. Such Fe3O4@SiO2@mSiO2-SiCDs nanoparticles solution exhibits blue light emission with a maximum wavelength of 470 nm at lower concentrations, and red-shifts to yellow-green light emission with maximum wavelength of 565 nm at higher concentrations, which is different with traditional carbon dots composite materials displaying strong emission only in the blue-light region. The SiCDs are immobilized in the outer mesoporous silica layer by covalent bonds via proper blending ration, which enables the nanoparticles solution to possess excellent fluorescence stability. The Fe3O4@SiO2@mSiO2-SiCDs nanoparticles show good stability, high surface area (483.19 m2/g), high saturation magnetization (22.1 emu/g) and low remanence (0.217 emu/g). When the concentration of nanoparticles solution is increased, the distance between the nanoparticles decreases and exhibits the concentration-mediated scattering phenomena, which result in the red-shift of fluorescent peaks. The specific structure of the Fe3O4@SiO2@mSiO2-SiCDs nanoparticles and the concentration-mediated scattering phenomena play key roles in the red-shift appearing.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Magnetic nanoparticles
Carbon dots
Composite silica material
Fluorescent properties
Red-shift

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of Fe3O4@SiO2@mSiO2 nanoparticles with immobilized SiCDs
Cell viability test by CCK-8 assay
Fluorescent properties of SiCDs and Fe3O4@SiO2@mSiO2-SiCDs nanoparticles
Phenomenon and mechanism of concentration-mediated red-shift
In vitro cytotoxicity characteristics and application

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 81571020 , 61376014 , 61775242 ) and the Natural Science Foundation of Guangdong Province (Nos. 2015A030313179 , 2014A030311050 ). The authors gratefully acknowledge the technical assistance from Prof. Jianhua Zhou, Dr. Le Yang, Dr. Jinyuan Li and Dr. Jingyuan Li.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Synthesis process of organosilane-functionalized carbon dots (SiCDs) immobilized silica composite nanoparticles (Fe3O4@SiO2@mSiO2-SiCDs) were prepared by a modified sol-gel method.

(a) TEM and HRTEM images of SiCDs. Inset shows the well dispersibility of SiCDs. (b) FTIR spectrum of SiCDs. (c) FESEM image of Fe3O4@SiO2@mSiO2-SiCDs nanoparticles. (d) TEM image of the core Fe3O4 particles. Inset is the SAED pattern of Fe3O4 particles.

TEM images of Fe3O4@SiO2 particles with different volume ratio of DI water and ethanol (short for R) in the synthesis process: (a)4:1; (b)2:1; (c)1:1; (d)1:2; (e)1:4. And TEM images of Fe3O4@SiO2@mSiO2-SiCDs nanoparticles with different amount of TEOS and ammonia solution in the synthesis process: (f)TEOS = 1 mL, NH3·H2O = 0.5 mL; (g) TEOS = 0.75 mL, NH3·H2O = 0.5 mL; (h) TEOS = 0.5 mL, NH3·H2O = 0.5 mL; (i) TEOS = 0.5 mL, NH3·H2O = 0.4 mL; (j) TEOS = 0.5 mL, NH3·H2O = 0.3 mL; (k) TEOS = 0.5 mL, NH3·H2O = 0.2 mL.

TEM and SEDS images of Fe3O4@SiO2@mSiO2-SiCDs nanoparticles with photo of the nanoparticles solution without and with fluorescence. (a) TEM image of Fe3O4@SiO2@mSiO2-SiCDs nanoparticles with great dispersity. (b) Photo of the nanoparticles solution without and with fluorescence. The left sample is the nanoparticles solution without excitation, and the right sample is the nanoparticles solution under λex = 360 nm; (c) High-resolution TEM image. The inner silica layer and the mesoporous silica layer can be easily distinguished. The black dots in the red circles refer to the SiCDs; (d) The HRTEM image. SiCDs are successfully immobilized in the mesoporous silica with a lattice spacing of 0.206 nm, which is consistent well with the HRTEM image of SiCDs; (e) Grey pattern; (f) Fe element; (g) Si element; (h) C element.

(a) XRD patterns of Fe3O4 nanoparticles and Fe3O4@SiO2@mSiO2-SiCDs nanoparticles. Inset is the low-angle XRD pattern of Fe3O4@SiO2@mSiO2-SiCDs nanoparticles; (b) Raman spectra of Fe3O4@SiO2@mSiO2 particles and Fe3O4@SiO2@mSiO2-SiCDs particles; (c) Nitrogen adsorption/desorption isotherm of Fe3O4@SiO2@mSiO2-SiCDs nanoparticles; and (d) Pore-size distribution curve of Fe3O4@SiO2@mSiO2-SiCDs nanoparticles.

XPS spectra of SiCDs nanoparticles: (a) Survey (b) C 1s; (c) O 1s; (d) Si 2p and (e) N 1s. And XPS spectra of Fe3O4@SiO2@mSiO2-SiCDs nanoparticles: (f) Survey (g) C 1s; (h) O 1s; (i) Si 2p and (j) N 1s.

(a) FTIR spectra of Fe3O4 particles, Fe3O4@SiO2 particles and Fe3O4@SiO2@mSiO2-SiCDs particles; (b) Magnetic hysteresis curves of Fe3O4 particles, Fe3O4@SiO2 particles and Fe3O4@SiO2@mSiO2-SiCDs particles; (c) The strong magnetic response of Fe3O4@SiO2@mSiO2-SiCDs particles solution under a low external magnetic field; (d) ζ-potential of Fe3O4 particles, Fe3O4@SiO2 particles and Fe3O4@SiO2@mSiO2-SiCDs particles.

(a) UV absorption spectra (Blue), fluorescent emission spectra (Black, λex = 360 nm) and fluorescent excitation spectra (Red, λem = 470 nm) of SiCDs solution (Solid lines) and Fe3O4@SiO2@mSiO2-SiCDs solution (Dotted lines, 130 μg/mL). (b) Stability of Fe3O4@SiO2@mSiO2-SiCDs solution in multi-types of metal cation solution. (c) Stability of Fe3O4@SiO2@mSiO2-SiCDs solution in multi-types of acid radical solution. (d) The change of the FL intensity of Fe3O4@SiO2@mSiO2-SiCDs solution at different pH values under λex = 360 nm. (e) Comparation of the stability during long-time storage in the form of powder and aqueous solution. (f) Stability of Fe3O4@SiO2@mSiO2-SiCDs solution under irradiation of UV light.

(a) Emission-excitation contour map of SiCDs; (b) Emission-excitation contour map of Fe3O4@SiO2@mSiO2-SiCDs nanoparticles solution with the concentration of 130 μg/mL. (c) Emission-concentration contour map of Fe3O4@SiO2@mSiO2-SiCDs nanoparticles solution with different concentrations. As the concentration increases, the fluorescent peak redshifts from 470 to 565 nm.

(a) Fluorescence digital photos of Fe3O4@SiO2@mSiO2-SiCDs nanoparticles solution with different concentrations (λex = 360 nm). The turbidity of the nanoparticles solution enhances as the concentration increases; (b) Fluorescence digital photos of Fe3O4@SiO2@mSiO2 nanoparticles and SiCDs mixed solution with different concentrations (λex = 360 nm). The turbidity of the mixed solution enhances as the concentration increases.

Fluorescent spectra of SiCDs and Fe3O4@SiO2@mSiO2-SiCDs nanoparticles solution with different concentrations; and the relationship between the concentration and the maximum FL intensity. (a) Fluorescent spectra of SiCDs and Fe3O4@SiO2@mSiO2-SiCDs nanoparticles solution with lower concentrations of 0–160 μg/mL. With the increase of the concentration, the FL intensity increases; (b) Relationship between the concentration and the maximum FL intensity of the curves in (a). With the increase of the concentration, the increases of FL intensity slow down; (c) Fluorescent spectra of Fe3O4@SiO2@mSiO2-SiCDs nanoparticles solution with concentrations of 170–245 μg/mL. The maximum value of the intensity-concentration ratio is located at 210 μg/mL; (d) Fluorescent spectra of nanoparticles solution with concentrations of 270–610 μg/mL. The FL intensity declines gradually with red-shift appearing; (e) Fluorescent spectra of nanoparticles solution with concentrations of 690–840 μg/mL. Red-shift effects exceed the initial fluorescent peak of Fe3O4@SiO2@mSiO2-SiCDs solution when the concentration is increased to 840 μg/mL; (f) Fluorescent spectra of nanoparticles solution with concentrations of 900–1500 μg/mL. λem is red-shifted to 565 nm with a reduction of the FL intensity when the concentration is increased to 1500 μg/mL.

(a) FL intensity spectra of SiCDs solution with different concentrations (λex = 360 nm). (b) FL intensity spectra of Fe3O4@SiO2@mSiO2 particles and SiCDs mixed solution with different concentrations (λex = 360 nm). Cell viability by CCK-8 assay. SCC-15 cells and MC3T3 cells are incubated with different concentrations (0, 25, 50, 100, 200, 400, 800, 1000, 1250 and 1500 μg/mL) of (c) Fe3O4@SiO2@mSiO2-SiCDs nanoparticles and (d) Fe3O4@SiO2 nanoparticles.

head.ce:title: Design of Fe3O4@SiO2@mSiO2-organosilane carbon dots nanoparticles: Synthesis and fluorescence red-shift properties with concentration dependence

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Orthodontics, Guanghua School of Stomatology, Hospital of Stomatology, Guangdong Provincial Key Laboratory of Stomatology, Sun Yat-sen University, Guangzhou 510055, People's Republic of China

Guangdong Provincial Key Laboratory of Photovoltaics Technologies

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Correspondence to: Y. Xu, No.56 Lingyuan West Road, Guanghua School of Stomatology, Hospital of Stomatology, Sun Yat-sen University, Guangzhou 510055, People's Republic of China.
Correspondence to: L. Zhuang, C501, Building of Engineering, East Campus (Higher Education Mega Center), Sun Yat-sen University, Guangzhou 510006, People's Republic of China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: C501, Building of Engineering, East Campus (Higher Education Mega Center)

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Organosilane-functionalized carbon dots (SiCDs) immobilized silica composite nanoparticles (Fe3O4@SiO2@mSiO2-SiCDs) with dually emissive properties have been prepared by a modified sol-gel method. Such Fe3O4@SiO2@mSiO2-SiCDs nanoparticles solution exhibits blue light emission with a maximum wavelength of 470 nm at lower concentrations, and red-shifts to yellow-green light emission with maximum wavelength of 565 nm at higher concentrations, which is different with traditional carbon dots composite materials displaying strong emission only in the blue-light region. The SiCDs are immobilized in the outer mesoporous silica layer by covalent bonds via proper blending ration, which enables the nanoparticles solution to possess excellent fluorescence stability. The Fe3O4@SiO2@mSiO2-SiCDs nanoparticles show good stability, high surface area (483.19 m2/g), high saturation magnetization (22.1 emu/g) and low remanence (0.217 emu/g). When the concentration of nanoparticles solution is increased, the distance between the nanoparticles decreases and exhibits the concentration-mediated scattering phenomena, which result in the red-shift of fluorescent peaks. The specific structure of the Fe3O4@SiO2@mSiO2-SiCDs nanoparticles and the concentration-mediated scattering phenomena play key roles in the red-shift appearing.

ce:section.ce:para: Luminance carbon dots (CDs) are one of burgeoning carbon nanomaterials in recent years with unique optical properties, good biocompatibility, great aqueous solubility and plentiful functional surface moieties [1–3]. Thus, those specific properties demonstrate a myriad of potential applications for CDs including sensing, catalysis, bio-imaging, and drug delivery [4–6].

Up to now, numerous of studies have focused on CDs acting as excellent imaging luminance probes. During the early years, pristine CDs were directly applied as sensory materials for metal ions detection [7,8]. Because of the hydroxyl and carboxyl groups on the functionalization surface, modified CDs can be used facile as pH sensors [9,10]. Sun et al. synthesized nitrogen and phosphorus co-doped carbon dots with blue and green emission colors applied as nitro-explosives sensors [11]. Ge et al. prepared sulfur-doped CDs with red emission as multimodal fluorescence, photoacoustic and thermal theranostics [12]. However, most of the probes consist of the pure or modified CDs. Although luminance probes above show features such as bright fluorescence, resistance to photobleaching, biocompatibility and low biotoxicity [13], the CDs were directly exposed within solvent yet, and the properties of CDs can be interfered by ions, proteins and pH values of the external environment.

To improve and tune those properties, integration of CDs with other functional groups, fluorophores or substrates is found to be facile and effective. Tian et al. designed CDs composite metal organic frameworks complex to improve the detection sensitivity and selectivity of the probe [14]. Tabrizi et al. developed a high sensitive visible light-driven photoelectrochemical aptasensor for shrimp allergen tropomyosin detection using graphitic carbon nitride-TiO2 nanocomposite [15]. Sahu et al. produced a luminescent silica nanotheranostics for multimodal imaging [16]. Zhang et al. reported the synthesis of mitochondria-targeting nanoplatform with fluorescent carbon dots for long time imaging [17]. Among these composite nanomaterials, the vast majority of the CDs are located on the surface of substrates by forming covalent bond or Van der Waals interaction at last step of synthesis. The luminance probes are still susceptible to be interfered, and the potential bio-interaction effect of CDs is still limiting their broad applications [18].

Moreover, most of the CDs induce emission of blue light which also restricts their practical applications in environmental and biology-relevant analysis because of the strong luminance background of bio-samples. And the potential luminance damage from biological tissues under UV excitation should not be ignored. Approaches for realizing long-wavelength and multicolor emission have been widely reported including surface state and size changing controlled by synthetic routes [19], chemical doping and modification [20], and solvatochromic effect [21]. However, all the approaches mentioned above are based on complicated chemical methods, and specific biotoxicity substances are possible to be introduced during the synthesis process.

Here, we demonstrate a facile route to fabricate dually emissive organosilane-functionalized carbon dots (SiCDs) composite nanoparticles with excellent stability and great biocompatibility. The design of this kind of nanoparticles is based on the concentration-dependent fluorescence shifting property in utilization of carbon dots or carbon dots composite materials, which has been neglected by people in the past decade. In this study, for the first time, the composite fluorescent nanoparticles with good dispersity is fabricated by immobilization of the SiCDs via proper blending ration. After the immobilization of fluorescent SiCDs in the outer silica layer, the Fe3O4 core-based nanoplatforms show a shift from blue emission light to green emission light, which is only related to the concentration of nanoparticles. The concentration-mediated scattering phenomena of concentration-dependent fluorescence shifting property of carbon dots composite materials is investigated and interpreted with calculation method for the first time. The proposed fluorescent composite nanoparticles exhibit high analytical performance in terms of surface area, stability, sensitivity and functionalization properties for the further applications of magnetic guided substance detection, drug delivery, fluorescence label and targeted therapy.

Anhydrous FeCl3, trisodium citrate dehydrate (Na3C6H5O7·2H2O), sodium acetate (NaAc), citric acid anhydrous, tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS), and ammonia solution (NH3·H2O, 28 wt%) were supplied by Aldrich-sigma. Ethylene glycol (EG), petroleum ether, methanol, ethanol, (3-aminopropyl) triethoxylsilane (APTES) and acetone were purchased from Shanghai Chemical Corp. (Shanghai, China). N-(β-aminoethyl)-γ-aminopropyl methyldimethoxy silane (AEAPMS), cetyltrimethyl ammonium bromide (CTAB) and cell counting kit-8 (CCK8) were obtained from Aladdin Chemical Corp. Ammonium nitrate (NH4NO3) were acquired from MOLBASE Institute of Biotechnology Corp. (Shanghai, China). The ultrathin carbon support film for TEM analysis was purchased from Guangzhou Zhongjingkeyi Technology Co., Ltd. Gases were bought from Airgas and used in the ultra-high purity (>99%). Deionized water was used for all experiments. All the reagents are of analytical grade and were used without further purification. Equipments for morphology and structure characterization are described in Supplemental Materials.

The hydrophobic Fe3O4 nanoparticles were prepared by high pressure hydrothermal method. Typically, anhydrous FeCl3 (0.325 g) and trisodium citrate (0.4 g) were dissolved in ethylene glycol (40 mL) under magnetic stirring until the forming of an orange-yellow solution. Sodium acetate (3.0 g) was added with magnetic stirring until obtaining a homogeneous yellow-brown solution. Then the solution was transferred into a 50 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and maintained at 200 °C for 10 h. After cooling down to room temperature, the black product was purified by ethanol 5 times with magnetic separation and further washed by deionized water one time and dried at 40 °C in vacuum.

Modified sol-gel method was used for the synthesis of Fe3O4@SiO2 nanoparticles. 0.15 g Fe3O4 nanoparticles, 10 mL DI water and 40 mL ethanol were mixed together with ultrasonic dispersion for 5 min and then transferred to a 250 mL three-neck round-bottom flask in water bath under vigorous mechanical stirring (1000 rpm). Afterwards, 0.714 mL concentrated ammonia solution and 2.5 mL TEOS (10% v/v in ethanol) were injected into the previous solution using micropipette and then mechanically stirred for 10 h at 30 °C. The black core-shell Fe3O4@SiO2 product was separated in ethanol and washed 3 times with magnetic separation, and finally dried at 40 °C in vacuum.

SiCDs were synthesized in N2 atmosphere at a high temperature condition. Firstly, 10 mL AEAPMS was placed into a 100 mL three necked-flask in an oil bath and kept at 220 °C for 5 min with effective reflux. Then 0.5 g anhydrous citric acid was rapidly added into the solution under vigorous stirring. An orange-yellow clear solution was obtained after further reaction for 1 min. The final product was purified by extraction with 50 mL petroleum for three times and then dissolved in 100 mL ethanol. Finally, the SiCDs dilute solution was obtained.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of Fe3O4@SiO2@mSiO2 nanoparticles with immobilized SiCDs
ce:section.ce:para: The dried Fe3O4@SiO2 particles were employed as seeds for growing a mesoporous silica shell coated on the silicon dioxide layer. At the beginning, 0.115 g Fe3O4@SiO2 particles were dissolved into 20 mL ethanol with ultrasonic dispersion for 10 min in a N2 atmosphere. 0.1125 g CTAB, 12 mL DI water and 28 mL ethanol were mixed in a 100 mL three necked-flask with medium mechanical stirring (600 rpm) until a clear solution was formed. The first solution was injected into the second solution, and then 2.5 mL SiCDs dilute solution. Following, 0.3 mL ammonia solution was added into the obtained solution. Finally, 0.5 mL TEOS was injected into the previous homogeneous solution with vigorous mechanical stirring (1000 rpm) for 2 h at 35 °C. The product was collected with a magnet and washed with ethanol and DI water about 4–5 times. To get rid of the CTAB, the washed product was refluxed with a NH4NO3 ethanol solution (0.6 wt% in ethanol), at 75 °C for 12 h. The Fe3O4@SiO2@mSiO2-SiCDs nanoparticles can be stable in ethanol for several months with good dispersity. Lyophilization (lasted for 24 h, reaching a vacuum under 133 μbar at −70 °C) was used to obtain the Fe3O4@SiO2@mSiO2-SiCDs powder, which can be stored at room temperature for the subsequent experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cell viability test by CCK-8 assay
ce:section.ce:para: Human non-small cell lung cancer cell line SCC-15 and MC3T3 cells (2 × 103 cells) were seeded into 96-well plates and incubated in 100 μL of DMEM: F12 (1:1) culture medium containing 10% FBS for 24 h (37 °C, 5% CO2). Then the cells were incubated with different concentrations of Fe3O4@SiO2 nanoparticles and Fe3O4@mSiO2-SiCDs nanoparticles (0, 25, 50, 100, 200, 400, 800, 1000, 1250 and 1500 μg/mL) in fresh DMEM: F12 (1:1) culture medium without serum for another 24 h, and after incubation viable cell concentration was checked by CCK8 assay. All the experiments were performed in triplicate.

The schematic diagram of the synthesis process of Fe3O4@SiO2@mSiO2-SiCDs nanoparticles is illustrated in Fig. 1 . TEM images, FTIR, and XPS characterization of the SiCDs are shown in Figs. 2a, b and 6f–j. TEM and HRTEM images clearly identify that SiCDs are spherical, monodisperse and have a narrow size distribution between 2.5 and 5.0 nm, and most of the SiCDs are 4.0 nm, while the lattice spacing of 0.207 nm which corresponds to the [102] facet of graphitic carbon. The inset in Fig. 2a shows the well dispersibility of SiCDs. The synthesis reaction system needs strict water-free and oxygen-free conditions [22,23]. Existence of water and oxygen influence greatly on the formation of SiCDs in this experiment. Water could suppress the amidation reaction, and oxygen in the synthesis process might partially oxidize the imine groups [24,25]. Either of these circumstances influences the fluorescent properties of SiCDs. The blue-green emissive Fe3O4@SiO2@mSiO2-SiCDs nanoparticles were obtained by a sol-gel synthetic route from TEOS and SiCDs.

The synthetic conditions of the two silica layers were investigated, especially including the initial volume ratio of reagents. At the beginning, Fe3O4@SiO2 nanoparticles are synthesized using the modified Stöber method. With the changing of the volume ratio of DI water and ethanol (short for R, from 4:1 to 1:4), the dispersity of Fe3O4@SiO2 particles shows much difference. Higher R value results in poor dispersity, since the Fe3O4@SiO2 particles are easier to aggregate owing to a hydrolysis acceleration effect and the changing of surface potential of the particles [26,27]. As the R value declines to 1:4, the Fe3O4@SiO2 nanoparticles show better dispersity with relatively smooth surface (Fig. 3a–e). And this volume ratio (1:4) is to be used in the following reactions.

During the preparation of Fe3O4@SiO2@mSiO2-SiCDs nanoparticles, TEOS acts as a silica source, while the SiCDs act as fluorescent part and CTAB acts as the structure-directing agent. By coexistence with the SiCDs, an unordered outer silica layer with random mesoporous channels was coated on the first SiO2 layer by a coating strategy in ethanol-water phase. As illustrated in Fig. 3f to g, the volume of TEOS is a vital factor for the synthesis of Fe3O4@SiO2@mSiO2-SiCDs nanoparticles. A higher TEOS concentration (for example 1.0 mL) could lead to much rapid hydrolysis and polymerization. Unreacted TEOS in the solution cause the forming of silica spheres (~80 nm diameter) which attach to the Fe3O4@SiO2@mSiO2-SiCDs nanoparticles disorderly (Fig. 3f–h). When the concentration of the TEOS is decreased to 0.5 mL, hydrolysis and polymerization slow down, and thereby the silica spheres are smaller and the surface of Fe3O4@SiO2@mSiO2-SiCDs particles is smoother. As illustrated in Fig. 3i and k, the volume of ammonia solution is another important factor affecting the dispersity of the Fe3O4@SiO2@mSiO2-SiCDs nanoparticles. It is found that 0.3 mL ammonia solution results in a best dispersity as well as a good morphology. If we increase the volume of ammonia solution to 0.4 mL, the final obtained solution looks much darker, which is due to the aggregation of the Fe3O4@SiO2 particles. A lower concentration of TEOS or ammonia solution may not obtain the final product either, since long-time continuous reaction might lead to the volatilization of ammonia and form reaction suspension.

Through contrasting experiments, we use Fe3O4@SiO2@mSiO2-SiCDs nanoparticles (Figs. 3j and 4 ) prepared with 0.5 mL TEOS and 0.3 mL ammonia solution per reaction due to their good dispersity, uniform shape and excellent fluorescent properties. The final product is brown powder and the reaction phenomena of different batches are consistent under same synthesis conditions.

Figs. 4a and 2c confirm that the Fe3O4@SiO2@mSiO2-SiCDs nanoparticles are nearly spherical and display a core-multilayer structure of about 600 nm thickness. The particles solution is of great dispersibility and strong fluorescence as shown in Fig. 4b. In Fig. 4c, the inner homogeneous silica shell (about 40 nm) and the outer mesoporous silica shell (about 100 nm) are clearly observed. SiCDs are immobilized in the mesoporous SiO2 layer. Ethanol dispersion method was used in the sample preparation for TEM analysis. The high-resolution TEM image of SiCDs in Fig. 2a reveals a clear fringe distance of 0.207 nm, which corresponds to the (102) lattice distance of graphite. The selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern of Fig. 2d indicates that the core Fe3O4 particles match the inverse spinel magnetite phase which is also confirmed in Fig. 5a. The scanning energy disperse spectroscopy (SEDS) of Fe3O4@SiO2@mSiO2-SiCDs nanoparticles in Fig. 4e–h shows the distribution of Fe, Si and C elements. By comparing the density of different elements in the images, the Fe3O4 core, the compact inner silica shell and the loose outer silica shell can be distinguished obviously. The mass distribution of C element in the outer mesoporous silica layer cannot be regarded as the immobilized SiCDs directly because of the background of ultrathin pure carbon film. Great dispersity of the Fe3O4@SiO2@mSiO2-SiCDs nanoparticles indicates that the Fe3O4@SiO2@mSiO2-SiCDs nanoparticles could be dispersed in aqueous solution as a good carrier in drug delivery system and could be existed as single nanoparticle with longer precipitation time.

Crystal structures and phase purity of Fe3O4@SiO2@mSiO2-SiCDs nanoparticles are determined by XRD. In the low-angle XRD pattern (see the inset of Fig. 5a), there is a strong diffraction peak from 0.5° to 3.8°, which represents a two-dimensional mesoporous structure of the outer silica layer. The broad weak peak from 3.8° to 8.0° may due to the non-uniform distribution of mesoporous and structural disorder due to the unordered immobilization process of SiCDs, which is in accordance with the reported mesoporous silica [16]. In the wide-angle XRD pattern in Fig. 5a, the diffraction peaks match well with the normal characteristic diffractions of the Fe3O4 inverse spinel structure (PCPDFWIN v.2.02, PDF No. 89-0691). The lattice parameter is calculated to be 8.38 A°. Besides the characteristic peaks of Fe3O4, the broad peak of Fe3O4@SiO2@mSiO2-SiCDs nanoparticles from 21° to 26° can be assigned to the amorphous silica shell (PCPDFWIN v.2.02, PDF No. 29-0085) and the disordered graphitic structure [28]. The presence of carbon dots cannot be affirmed according to the XRD pattern only.

The Raman spectrum of Fe3O4@SiO2@mSiO2-SiCDs nanoparticles shown in Fig. 5b confirms the immobilization of SiCDs on the silica-coated magnetite nanoparticles. Two characteristic vibrations at 1375 cm−1 (D band) and 1570 cm−1 (G band) indicate the CC bond of SiCDs. The G band is attributed to the vibration of sp2 bonded carbon atoms in a two-dimensional hexagonal lattice, while the D band is associated with the vibration of carbon atoms with dangling bonds in the plane terminations of the disordered graphite. Because of the covalent immobilization of SiCDs, the dangling bonds outside the SiCDs decline which results in a broader D-band. The ID/IG ratio is found to be 1.35, which is higher than those of the reported carbon dots [29,30]. Thus, the relatively high D-band intensity may be due to the presence of abundant oxygen-containing groups on the surface of Fe3O4@SiO2@mSiO2-SiCDs particles [31]. Moreover, the sharp peaks at 685, 501 and 338 cm−1 correspond to the magnetite core [32].

The nitrogen adsorption/desorption isotherm of Fe3O4@SiO2@mSiO2-SiCDs particles exhibit the type IV isotherm curves with hysteresis (Fig. 5c and d), which is a characteristic of mesoporous materials. The surface area and the total pore volume per gram are calculated to be as high as 483.19 m2/g and 0.591 cm3/g. Pore-size distribution determined from the desorption part of the isotherm confirms the formation of the mesoporous SiO2 with an average diameter of 3 nm. The nitrogen adsorption/desorption isotherm analysis shows the mesoporous silica shell with high surface area, which provides large combination site for further modification.

To further study the surface functional groups and composition of the SiCDs and Fe3O4@SiO2@mSiO2-SiCDs particles, we carried out XPS survey. The peaks at 100–106, 282–290, 396–404 and 528–536 eV in Fig. 6a refer to the binding energy of Si 2p (b), C 1s (c), N 1s (d) and O 1s (e) with the atomic percentage of 1.46, 60.99, 10.3 and 27.02, respectively. In Fig. 6b, fitting result displays that the curve of C1s consists of four peaks centered at 284.3, 284.8, 286.1, 287.6 eV and 288.4 eV which corresponds to the CC (sp2 carbon), CC/CH (sp3 carbon), CN/CO (sp3 carbon), the CONH (oxidized carbon) and COOH (carboxyl group), respectively. And in Fig. 6c, the broad peaks of high-resolution O 1s photoemission at 530.7 and 532.8 eV correspond to CO and SiO, respectively, while the strong peak at 531.5 eV refers to the OH of carboxyl group and silicon hydroxyl. In Fig. 6d, multiple Si 2p photoemission image represents for the corresponding SiC bond at 101.7 eV and SiO bond at 103.2 eV. The intensity of SiC bond is much higher than SiO bond, which suggests the methoxysilane on the surface of SiCDs. In Fig. 6e, 399.6 eV and 401.0 eV are speculated to pyrrolic N (NH) and potential graphitic N (CN) of SiCDs, and the peak appearing at 401.7 eV is attributed to nitrogen in -CONH- bond. In Fig. 6f, the peaks at 100–106, 280–290, 396–404 and 528–538 eV refer to the binding energy of Si 2p, C 1s, N 1s and O 1s with the atomic percentage of 13.98, 46.78, 4.39 and 31.79, respectively. In Fig. 6g, fitting result displays that the curve of C 1s consists of four peaks centered at 284.3, 284.8, 286.1 and 288.4 eV which corresponds to the CC (sp2 carbon), CC/CH (sp3 carbon), C-N/C-O (sp3 carbon) and COOH/CONH (oxidized carbon), respectively [33]. In Fig. 6h, broad peaks of high-resolution O 1s photoemission at 531.0 and 532.8 eV correspond to CO and SiO, respectively, while the strong peak at 531.9 eV refers to the OH of the outer mesoporous silica shell. In Fig. 6i, multiple Si 2p photoemission image represents for the corresponding SiC bond at 102.0 eV and SiO bond at 103.2 eV. In Fig. 6j, the high-resolution scan of the N 1s can be fitted to three peaks of NH, CN and CONH bonds. 399.6 eV and 401.0 eV are speculated to pyrrolic N (NH) and graphitic N (CN) of SiCDs in the silica shell, which is identical with the bonds of SiCDs in Fig. 6e. The peak appearing at 401.9 eV is attributed to nitrogen in CONH bond [33,34].

FTIR spectrum illustrates the bonding composition and functional groups of SiCDs in Fig. 2a. Sharp peak at 1650 cm−1 for SiCDs is attributed to aromatic CC stretching, which is the elementary unit of the carbon dots. The appearance of the broad peak centering at 3200–3500 cm−1 (OH vibrational stretch), a sharp peak at 1735 cm−1 (CO vibrational stretch) and peaks at 1056 and 1084 cm−1 (COC/CN vibrational stretch) suggest the existence of carboxylate and hydroxyl groups on the surface of SiCDs. Because of the addition of AEAPMS, the sharp peaks at 954 and 465 cm−1 represent for the SiOH and SiO bonds, respectively. In addition, the sharp broad peak from 2600 to 2750 cm−1 of CH bond confirms the successful connection of long-chain CH2 of AEAPMS. The FTIR spectra of Fe3O4 particles, Fe3O4@SiO2 particles and Fe3O4@SiO2@mSiO2-SiCDs particles are shown in Fig. 7a. The strong absorption at 557 cm−1 is accounted for FeO bond of the Fe3O4 core. The bonds around 1000–1200, 954 and 456 cm−1 correspond to the vibrations of SiOSi, SiOH and SiO, respectively. Characteristic peaks at 3200–3400, 2882 and 1725 cm−1 represent the typical stretching vibrations of OH, NH and CO bonds. These results are consistent well with the XPS survey, revealing that SiCDs have been immobilized successfully in the mesoporous silica layer, and there are many hydrophilic functional groups on the surface of Fe3O4@SiO2@mSiO2-SiCDs particles. The FTIR and XPS analysis reveal that amounts of hydrophilic functional groups exist on the surface of Fe3O4@SiO2@mSiO2-SiCDs nanoparticles, which refers to the easiness and convenience for further combination of different drugs in drug delivery system.

Magnetic hysteresis curves in Fig. 7b indicate that the intermediate products and the final product are desirable superparamagnetic at 300 K, which is a satisfactory property for magnetic separation, fluorescent probe, targeted drug delivery and other applications. The saturation magnetization is measured to be 59.6, 39.2 and 22.1 emu/g for the Fe3O4, the Fe3O4@SiO2 and the Fe3O4@SiO2@mSiO2-SiCDs nanoparticles, respectively. The magnetization rapidly increases with the applied field up to 3000 Oe and reaches the saturation value at 5000 Oe. Because of the superparamagnetic property and the high saturation magnetization, Fe3O4@SiO2@mSiO2-SiCDs nanoparticles demonstrate the strong magnetic sensitivity of the probe by lower external magnetic fields (as shown in Fig. 7c and Supplementary video 1). The superparamagnetic property and high saturation magnetization of Fe3O4@SiO2@mSiO2-SiCDs nanoparticles are benefit for recycle and reuse in application.

Magnetic hysteresis curves in Fig. 7b indicate that the intermediate products and the final product are desirable superparamagnetic at 300 K, which is a satisfactory property for magnetic separation, fluorescent probe, targeted drug delivery and other applications. The saturation magnetization is measured to be 59.6, 39.2 and 22.1 emu/g for the Fe3O4, the Fe3O4@SiO2 and the Fe3O4@SiO2@mSiO2-SiCDs nanoparticles, respectively. The magnetization rapidly increases with the applied field up to 3000 Oe and reaches the saturation value at 5000 Oe. Because of the superparamagnetic property and the high saturation magnetization, Fe3O4@SiO2@mSiO2-SiCDs nanoparticles demonstrate the strong magnetic sensitivity of the probe by lower external magnetic fields (as shown in Fig. 7c and Supplementary video 1). The superparamagnetic property and high saturation magnetization of Fe3O4@SiO2@mSiO2-SiCDs nanoparticles are benefit for recycle and reuse in application.

The ζ-potential analysis of Fig. 7d reveals the modification during the establishment process of Fe3O4@SiO2@mSiO2-SiCDs particles. Owing to the surface modification by trisodium citrate dehydrate, the Fe3O4 core shows a negative potential of −25.4 mV. Fe3O4@SiO2 particles display a high negative potential of −41.3 mV which is accounted for the surface Si-OH groups. And the Fe3O4@SiO2@mSiO2-SiCDs particles display a positive potential of 41.5 mV, suggesting the existence of amino on the surface of the SiCDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent properties of SiCDs and Fe3O4@SiO2@mSiO2-SiCDs nanoparticles
ce:section.ce:para: We further investigate the fluorescent properties of SiCDs and Fe3O4@SiO2@mSiO2-SiCDs particles, including UV absorption spectra, fluorescence spectra and fluorescence stability. As shown in Fig. 8a, SiCDs and Fe3O4@SiO2@mSiO2-SiCDs particles dispersed in water indicate two characteristic peaks at around 235 nm and 345 nm, which are due to the trapping of the π–π* transitions of aromatic sp2 domains and n–π transitions by the surface states, respectively [22,33]. SiCDs show strong blue emission in Fig. 9a under UV illumination. With increasing the wavelength from 300 to 440 nm, obvious red-shift of the emission peak from 470 to 500 nm cannot be observed. The excitation-dependent emission properties are possibly attributed to variable surface excited states [35]. Meanwhile, fluorescent properties of Fe3O4@SiO2@mSiO2-SiCDs particles in low concentration show similar characteristics with the SiCDs but a lower fluorescent intensity (FL intensity, as shown in Fig. 9b). The strongest fluorescent emission of SiCDs and Fe3O4@SiO2@mSiO2-SiCDs particles are both located at λem = 470 nm (a bright blue emission) upon a 375 nm excitation. The full width at half maximum of the emission peak is about 75 nm, revealing a narrow size distribution of the particles.

Different from the SiCDs or the reported carbon dots, in our case, Fe3O4@SiO2@mSiO2-SiCDs particles is much more stable in multi-types of salt solution and own the different fluorescent quenching properties (as shown in Fig. 8b–f), which indicates that the covalent attachment between SiCDs and the outer mesoporous silica layer is benefit for the good stability of fluorescence in complex solutions. In addition, the stability of Fe3O4@SiO2@mSiO2-SiCDs particles in acidic and alkaline solutions is tested. Fig. 8d depicts the change of the fluorescent intensity of Fe3O4@SiO2@mSiO2-SiCDs solution at different pH values under 360 nm excitation. It is seen that an increase of pH from 1 to 6 leads to an increase of the fluorescent intensity and the pH from 6 to 8 appears to be a balanced stage, and continually increasing the pH to 14 results in a gradual decrease of the intensity. The pH sensibility related to the surface protonation and deprotonation of our SiCDs is weaker than those reported carbon dots [33,36,37]. The stability of Fe3O4@SiO2@mSiO2-SiCDs particles is benefit for potential application in complex environment and drug delivery systems.

It is not a negligible issue that the Fe3O4@SiO2@mSiO2-SiCDs nanoparticles show good stability during long-time storage in the form of powder, while the particles are not stable in aqueous solution. In Fig. 8e, the FL intensity of the nanoparticles in aqueous solution decreases 15% after 18 days storage. As shown in Fig. 8f, the fluorescent intensity of the solution decreases slightly and preserves about 91% of the initial intensity after 90 min irradiation by UV light. It is speculated that the surface state of Fe3O4@SiO2@mSiO2-SiCDs particles and SiCDs cannot be decomposed in the form of powder, while some imine groups on the surface of the SiCDs may be partially oxidized by light or oxygen in aqueous solutions and thus affect the fluorescent intensity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Phenomenon and mechanism of concentration-mediated red-shift
ce:section.ce:para:
In the synthesis of Fe3O4@SiO2@mSiO2-SiCDs particles, three reactions proceed in the sol-gel process at the functional group level [38]:
≡ Si − O
CH 3
+
H 2
O
⇒ hydrolysis
≡ Si − OH +
CH 3
OH
≡ Si − O
CH 3
+ HO − Si ≡
⇒ alcohol condensation
≡ Si − O − Si ≡ +
CH 3
OH
≡ Si − OH + HO − Si ≡
⇒ water condensation
≡ Si − O − Si ≡ +
H 2
O

With the hydrolyzing of methoxy groups, organosilane-functionalized carbon dots act as a kind of silicon source and are immobilized in the mesoporous silica layer accompanying the reactions of sol-gel process. We suggest that the vast majority of immobilization reactions happen between the methoxy groups of SiCDs and the silanol groups of silica layer and result in the covalent linkage. Thus, the fluorescent properties of monomer SiCDs with characteristic surface states are not changed and show similar peak positions of the strongest fluorescent emission.

However, to our surprise, different concentrations of Fe3O4@SiO2@mSiO2-SiCDs aqueous solution lead to different fluorescent properties. The emission-concentration contour map of the solution with different concentrations of 0–1600 μg/mL are exhibited in Fig. 9c, respectively. As the concentration increases, the fluorescent peak varies from 470 to 565 nm and displays a fluorescence red-shift, while the FL intensity of the solution increases first and then decreases. As shown in Fig. 10 , the turbidity of the nanoparticles solution and the mixed solution enhances as the concentration increases. Hence, the red-shift of fluorescent peaks is related to the scattering effects of nanoparticles in high concentrations. When the concentration is in the range of 0–160 μg/mL, the unimodal emission peak of Fe3O4@SiO2@mSiO2-SiCDs aqueous solution is about 470 nm which is consistent with the emission peak of monomer SiCDs as shown in Fig. 11a. And the distance between individual Fe3O4@SiO2@mSiO2-SiCDs nanoparticles in the solution is much larger than its diameter (detailed calculations are given in the Supplementary Material). Thus, the scattering of particles' emission light can be ignored. While with the increase of the concentration, the increases of FL intensity slow down in Fig. 11b, which is mainly caused by the enhancement of solution turbidity. In Fig. 11c, the FL intensity varies little with the concentration ranges from 170 to 245 μg/mL, and the maximum value of the intensity-concentration ratio is located at 210 μg/mL. This concentration range is possibly attributed to the balance of the emission and the solution turbidity. Meanwhile there are no red-shifts of fluorescent peaks suggesting that the scattering is not obvious either. However, when the concentration is further increased, the FL intensity declines gradually with red-shift appearing in Fig. 11d. In Fig. 11e, as the concentration is increased to 840 μg/mL, red-shift effects exceed the initial fluorescent peak of Fe3O4@SiO2@mSiO2-SiCDs solution. The solution turns to emit uniform yellow-green light. In Fig. 11f, when the concentration is increased to 1500 μg/mL, the λem is red-shifted to 565 nm with a reduction of the FL intensity.

Although the mechanism of red-shift is not clear, we suggest the possible reasons for specific structure of the nanoparticles in Fig. 1 and concentration-mediated scattering phenomena. We hypothesize that Fe3O4@SiO2@mSiO2-SiCDs nanoparticles show monodispersity, regular morphology and well-distribution in aqueous solution. The nitrogen adsorption/desorption isotherm of Fe3O4@SiO2@mSiO2-SiCDs nanoparticles exhibits the total pore volume per gram of 0.591 cm3/g. From TEM image, the radius of Fe3O4 core is100 nm (r0). The thickness of the inner silica layer and outer silica layer is 50 nm (d1) and 100 nm (d2), respectively. The volume of pore within a nanoparticle, volume of silica within a nanoparticle, volume of inner silica layer within a nanoparticle and volume of outer silica layer within a nanoparticle are abbreviated to be V pore , V silica , V inner and V outer , respectively. The density of Fe3O4 particles and SiO2 particles is 5.17 g/cm3 and 2.2 g/cm3, respectively. When we ignore the mass of the immobilized SiCDs, the mass of single nanoparticle (m0) can be calculated as below:
m 0
= m
Fe 3
O 4
+ m
Inner silica layer
+ m
Outer silica layer
= ρ
Fe 3
O 4
× V
Fe 3
O 4
+ ρ
Si
O 2
×
V inner
+ ρ
Si
O 2
×
V outer
= ρ
Fe 3
O 4
× V
Fe 3
O 4
+ ρ
Si
O 2
×
V inner
+ ρ
Si
O 2
×
V silica
−
V pore
= ρ
Fe 3
O 4
×
4 3
π
r 0 3
+ ρ
Si
O 2
×
4 3
π
r 0
+
d 1
3
−
r 0 3
+ ρ
Si
O 2
×
4 3
π
r 0
+
d 1
+
d 2
3
−
r 0
+
d 1
3
− ρ
Si
O 2
×
V pore
= ρ
Fe 3
O 4
×
4 3
π
r 0 3
+ + ρ
Si
O 2
×
4 3
π
r 0
+
d 1
+
d 2
3
−
r 0 3
− ρ
Si
O 2
×
V pore
= 5.17 ×
4 3
π ×
1 ×
10
− 5
3
+ 2.2 ×
4 3
π
2.5 ×
10
− 5
3
−
1 ×
10
− 5
3
− 2.2 × 0.591 ×
m 0
Thus, the mass of single nanoparticle (m0) is calculated to be 1.2 × 10−13g.

Then, we hypothesize that the concentration of Fe3O4@SiO2@mSiO2-SiCDs nanoparticles is c0 (μg/mL), so the total number of nanoparticles in 1 mL aqueous solution (N0) is:
N 0
=
c 0
×
10
− 6
× 1
m 0
=
c 0
m 0
×
10
− 6
when the concentration raises to the highest value of 1500 μg/mL,
N 0
=
1500
1.2 ×
10
− 13
×
10
− 6
= 1.25 ×
10 10
, thus the distance between two adjacent nanoparticles (d) is:
d =
1 ×
10
− 6
N 0
3
≈ 0.431 ×
10
− 5
m = 4310
nm .

As calculated above, with increasing the concentration of Fe3O4@SiO2@mSiO2-SiCDs particles in aqueous solution, the distance between individual particles decreases. The immobilized SiCDs exist unordered in the outer silica layer, thus the Fe3O4@SiO2@mSiO2-SiCDs nanoparticles act as independent fluorescent units. As the concentration is increased to 1500 μg/mL, the distance between individual fluorescent units declines to 4310 nm. The ratio of the distance to the diameter of particles enable that we can ignore the correlated scattering between the particles [39]. When the excitation wavelength λex is 360 nm, the λem of 470 nm without scattering is approximately equal to the diameter of the particles. Thus, Mie scattering is activated [40–42] and those longwave emission lights are scattered at λem = 565 nm. The total fluorescence of the Fe3O4@SiO2@mSiO2-SiCDs aqueous solution is red-shifted. As for the SiCDs aqueous solution, the diameter of SiCDs is much less than the distance between the SiCDs particles and the emission wavelength λem is also 470 nm with an excitation light of 360 nm in Fig. 12a. Thus, Rayleigh scattering is activated and those shortwave emission lights are easier to be scattered [43–45]. However, the scattering is covered by their own bright blue emission light of the SiCDs.

To further explore the possible mechanism, Fe3O4@SiO2@mSiO2 particles and SiCDs mixed solution is studied as another comparation experiment in Fig. 12b. In the mixture solution, SiCDs exist among the Fe3O4@SiO2@mSiO2 particles and the mixture solution can be regarded as one fluorescence body. Even the SiCDs with diameter of about 4 nm, the scattering is also covered by the luminescence of the SiCDs. No obvious changes of the fluorescent properties are observed when the concentration of the mixture solution is changed. Thus, the specific structure of our Fe3O4@SiO2@mSiO2-SiCDs nanoparticles is an important influence factor on the redshift of the fluorescent properties.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro cytotoxicity characteristics and application
ce:section.ce:para: The cytotoxicity of the Fe3O4@SiO2@mSiO2-SiCDs nanoparticles (Fig. 12c) and Fe3O4@SiO2 nanoparticles (Fig. 12d) was evaluated in SCC-15 and MC3T3 cells. There is no significant drop in cell viability upon the addition of the nanoparticles up to 1500 μg/mL over an incubation period of 24 h. Thus, Fe3O4@SiO2@mSiO2-SiCDs nanoparticles are with great biocompatibility and can be explored as bioimaging sensor inside the living cell.

Potential applications are supported by the cell viability test (Fig. 12c and d), which show the great biocompatibility of the nanoparticles. Thus, with the uptake of Fe3O4@SiO2@mSiO2-SiCDs nanoparticles (or the Fe3O4@SiO2@mSiO2-SiCDs nanoparticles with further modification) by co-culture, the concentration of nanoparticles in cytoplasmic matrix or cell organelles can be easily detected at λex = 360 nm according to the concentration-dependent fluorescence shifting property. Hence, when the nanoparticles are combined with drugs, the intake of drugs can be calculated from the concentration-dependent fluorescence shifting phenomenon. Even more, after the further study about the regularity of cell uptake, different kinds of cell act out different intake property, which can be the mechanism of fluorescence sensor in cell counting. All the applications are based on the structure foundation and great biocompatibility of the nanoparticles.

In summary, we have fabricated dually emissive SiCDs composite nanoparticles with excellent fluorescent properties which show uniform morphology and good dispersibility in aqueous solution. The Fe3O4@SiO2@mSiO2-SiCDs nanoparticles solution exhibits blue light emission with a maximum wavelength of 470 nm at lower concentrations, and red-shifts to yellow-green light emission with maximum wavelength of 565 nm at higher concentrations. The Fe3O4@SiO2@mSiO2-SiCDs nanoparticles show good stability, high surface area (483.19 m2/g), high saturation magnetization (22.1 emu/g) and low remanence (0.217 emu/g). Through investigation of the preparation parameters and influence factors in the synthesis process by different characterization techniques, we give an optimized experimental scheme of the Fe3O4@SiO2@mSiO2-SiCDs nanoparticles. This is the first report to successfully immobilize the SiCDs in mesoporous silica shell, and the first effort to investigate the mechanism of concentration-dependent fluorescence shifting property of carbon dots composite materials. Fluorescence shifting property of our dually emissive SiCDs composite nanoparticles could be one important application since the fluorescence shifting can be initiated by the variation of the concentration easily rather than changing the specific complex conditions, which greatly simplifies the process needed in further applications. As the concentration of the nanoparticles solution increases, the fluorescent peak varies from 470 to 565 nm and displays a fluorescence red-shift, while the FL intensity of the solution increases first and then decreases. With calculation and analyses of the fluorescent properties of nanoparticles solution with different concentrations, we have proposed that the specific structure of Fe3O4@SiO2@mSiO2-SiCDs nanoparticles and the concentration-mediated scattering phenomena play key roles in the red-shift of fluorescent peaks, which could contribute to the potential applications in sensors and drug delivery carriers.

The following are the supplementary data related to this article.
Supplementary video 1
The strong magnetic sensitivity and great dispersibility of Fe3O4@SiO2@mSiO2-SiCDs nanoparticles by lower external magnetic fields.
Supplementary video 1
Supplementary material
Equipments for morphology and details about preparing the sample for TEM analysis.
Image 1
Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.matdes.2018.04.051.

This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 81571020, 61376014, 61775242) and the Natural Science Foundation of Guangdong Province (Nos. 2015A030313179, 2014A030311050). The authors gratefully acknowledge the technical assistance from Prof. Jianhua Zhou, Dr. Le Yang, Dr. Jinyuan Li and Dr. Jingyuan Li.
The raw/processed data required to reproduce these findings cannot be shared on line at this time as the data also forms part of an ongoing study. Data will be made available on request.
